1: If anyone sins in that he hears a public adjuration (qol alah) to testify and though he is a witness, whether he has seen or come to know the matter, yet does not speak — the qol alah (voice/announcement of an oath) is a formal public summons to witnesses to come forward in a legal proceeding; failure to testify when one has relevant knowledge creates a specific category of communal guilt (avon). The obligation to testify truthfully is foundational in the ancient Israelite legal system (Exodus 20:16; Leviticus 23:1-3), and non-testimony is here treated as a legal and cultic offense requiring atonement.
2: Or if anyone touches any unclean thing, whether a carcass of an unclean wild animal or a carcass of unclean livestock or a carcass of unclean swarming things (sheqetz), even though he does not know it — unintentional contact with animal carcasses (Lev 11 defines the clean/unclean taxonomy) creates culpability even when the person was unaware at the time. The concept of inadvertent defilement (nefesh ki tiggah) reflects an understanding that impurity has objective effects regardless of subjective intention, a feature of Israelite purity theology that distinguishes it from moral-intent-based systems like the Roman mens rea framework.
3: Or if he touches human uncleanness, of whatever sort the uncleanness may be, with which one becomes unclean (tum'at adam), and it is hidden from him — human impurity sources (corpse, discharge, skin disease) are distinguished from animal impurity sources (Leviticus 5:2); tum'at adam encompasses the full range of Lev 11–15 human impurity categories. The formula vene'elam mimenu (it was hidden from him) is the technical term for the unwitting aspect of these violations throughout Lev 4–5.
4: Or if anyone utters with his lips a rash oath (nishba levatte bisetafayim) to do evil or to do good, any sort of rash oath that people swear, and it is hidden from him — the rash oath (shevuat shav) is an impulsive, unconsidered verbal commitment later forgotten; the inclusion of oaths to do good (not only harmful oaths) indicates that the culpability is in the making-and-forgetting rather than in the content. The pairing 'evil or good' creates a merism covering all possible oath content.
5: When he becomes guilty in any of these, he shall confess the sin he has committed (ve-hitvaddah asher chata aleiha) — the confession requirement (viduy) precedes the offering; the ritual alone without acknowledgment of specific wrongdoing is insufficient. Confession before sacrifice is a distinctive feature of Israelite atonement theology; in Mesopotamian ritual texts (Marduk-šum-uṣur incantations, namburbi rituals) the verbal component exists but is typically addressed to deities in third person rather than first-person confession of specific acts.
6: He shall bring to YHWH as his compensation for the sin that he has committed, a female from the flock, a lamb or a goat, for a sin offering (chatat); and the priest shall make atonement for him for his sin — the standard chatat for these three categories is a female animal (contrast the male animal required for leaders and priests in Leviticus 4:3,22-23). The female animal represents the base-level offering for individual lay Israelites; the graduated scale that follows (Leviticus 5:7-13) accommodates economic inability to provide even this.
7: But if he cannot afford a lamb (im lo taggiya yado dei she), then he shall bring to YHWH as his compensation for the sin that he has committed two turtledoves or two pigeons (shtei torim o shtei vnei yona) — the sliding scale begins: those who cannot afford a sheep bring two birds instead. This graduated provision for the poor is one of the most distinctive features of the Levitical offering system; no comparable sliding scale appears in Mesopotamian temple records from Ur III or Old Babylonian periods, where temple offerings were largely fixed by professional category and social rank.
8: And he shall bring them to the priest, who shall offer first the one for the sin offering (chatat). He shall wring its head from its neck but shall not sever it completely (malaq et-rosho mimul orfo ve-lo yavdil) — bird sin offering technique: the priest wrings the bird's neck incompletely (meliqa), a method unique to bird offerings and distinct from the slaughter (shechita) used for quadrupeds. The blood is then daubed on the altar (Leviticus 5:9) rather than sprinkled or poured as with larger animals.
9: He shall sprinkle some of the blood of the sin offering on the side of the altar, and the rest of the blood shall be drained out at the base of the altar — the bird's smaller blood volume means only partial sprinkling on the altar side, with the remainder drained at the base; the base-drainage parallels the quadruped sin offering procedure where blood is poured at the altar base after the manipulations (Leviticus 4:25,30,34).
10: The second he shall offer as a burnt offering according to the rule — the second bird serves as a burnt offering after the sin offering bird; the pairing (sin offering + burnt offering) from two birds is an economic version of the two-animal offering given by the wealthy person. The sequence sin offering before burnt offering is standard throughout Leviticus (cf. Leviticus 14:19-20; Numbers 6:16-17).
11: But if he cannot afford two turtledoves or two pigeons (im lo taggiya yado lishetei torim), then he who sinned shall bring for his offering a tenth of an ephah of fine flour (issaron solet) for a sin offering — the second tier of graduation: those who cannot afford even two birds bring 1/10 ephah (~2.3 liters) of fine flour. The flour offering without oil or frankincense (Leviticus 5:11b) marks it as penitential rather than celebratory; normal grain offerings include oil (Leviticus 2:4-7) and frankincense (Leviticus 2:15).
12: He shall bring it to the priest, and the priest shall take a handful of it as its memorial portion (azkaratah) and burn this on the altar, on top of the LORD's food offerings — the handful (kometz) burned as the azkarah (memorial portion) is the standard grain offering procedure (Leviticus 2:2), applied here to the flour chatat. The remainder goes to the priest (Leviticus 5:13). The azkarah (memorial/token) represents the whole offering presented to YHWH; the priestly portion represents the priest's share in the atonement transaction.
13: Thus the priest shall make atonement for him for the sin that he has committed in any one of these things, and he shall be forgiven. And the remainder shall be for the priest, as in the grain offering — the atonement-and-forgiveness formula is identical whether the person brought a lamb, two birds, or flour; the economic tier does not diminish the efficacy of the atonement. The graduated scale is arguably the most explicitly compassionate provision in the entire sacrificial system, ensuring that poverty is no barrier to atonement.
14: YHWH spoke to Moses, saying — a new revelation formula separates the guilt offering (asham) legislation (Leviticus 5:14-19) from the graduated sin offering (Leviticus 5:1-13). The asham is a distinct sacrifice type from the chatat with different legal triggers and different restitution requirements.
15: If anyone commits a breach of faith (ma'al) and sins unintentionally in any of the holy things of YHWH (miqdeshei YHWH), he shall bring to YHWH as his compensation (ashmato) a ram without blemish from the flock, valued at silver shekels — the asham is triggered by ma'al (sacrilege, breach of faith against holy things), the failure to give YHWH what is rightly his: tithes, firstfruits, vow payments, priestly dues. The requirement for a ram of silver valuation distinguishes the asham from the chatat; a monetary equivalence must be established, reflecting the reparation (rather than mere atonement) character of the guilt offering.
16: He shall also make restitution for what he has done amiss in the holy thing and shall add a fifth to it (yosif chamishito alav) and give it to the priest — the chomesh (one-fifth surcharge on restitution) is distinctive to the asham system; no sliding scale applies here because the violation involved specific sacred property with assessable value. In Hammurabi's Code (§§5, 120, 124), restitution requirements vary from two-fold to ten-fold; the Levitical one-fifth surcharge is more modest but applied consistently across all asham contexts. The surcharge goes to the priest as part of the sacred property system.
17: If anyone sins, doing any of the things that by YHWH's commandments ought not to be done, though he did not know it (ve-hu lo yada), and realizes his guilt (ve-ashem), he shall bear his iniquity — the guilt offering for general unwitting violations (not limited to holy things as in Leviticus 5:15): the person who later realizes they may have transgressed brings the asham as a precautionary measure. This 'floating guilt' concept has no direct ANE parallel; Mesopotamian texts address known violations rather than suspected-but-uncertain ones.
18: He shall bring to the priest a ram without blemish from the flock, or its equivalent, for a guilt offering. The priest shall make atonement for him for the mistake that he made unintentionally, and he shall be forgiven — the same ram requirement as Leviticus 5:15; the shegagah (error, mistake) term used here and in Leviticus 4:2 covers the full range of unintentional violations. The asham for unwitting violation in Leviticus 5:17-19 (the 'doubtful asham') was extensively developed in rabbinic law as the asham talui (suspended guilt offering).
19: It is a guilt offering (asham hu); he has indeed incurred guilt before YHWH — the declaration asham hu formally categorizes the offering and confirms the person's legal status as guilty. The emphatic infinitive absolute pattern (asham asham in the Hebrew sense) reinforces the certainty of guilt even in the case of uncertainty about the specific act.
